title: Irregular Cycles, Subclinical Hypothyroidism, and a 4.5 cm Adnexal Cyst in VWD Type 2
subtitle: A Resident Training Module (RTM-21/female): TSH 10 with PRL 54 — treat LT4 first and defer pooled prolactin; simple cyst expectant management; LH:FSH >2 and 13 kg weight gain; Meprate bleeding risk and cyst rupture in VWD type 2; staged steroid panel only after thyroid and prolactin normalize
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Irregular menstrual cycles (delayed up to 2–3 months); responded to medroxyprogesterone (Meprate)
pmh: TSH 10 mIU/L with normal T3 and T4 — subclinical hypothyroidism; no goiter
Weight increased from 50 kg to 63 kg over 6 months (~13 kg gain)
Right adnexal anechoic cyst 4.5 cm abutting right ovary on ultrasound
Tumor markers negative: AFP 1.18, CEA, CA-125, CA 19.9
Von Willebrand disease type 2 — history of easy bruisability
medications: Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily — planned/initiated per faculty plan

caseDescription: A 21-year-old woman with irregular cycles (delayed 2–3 months, responding to Meprate), TSH 10 with normal T3/T4, prolactin 54, low FSH 2.7 and LH 5.9 (ratio >2), 13 kg weight gain in 6 months (50→63 kg), and a 4.5 cm right adnexal anechoic simple cyst with negative tumor markers. Known VWD type 2 with easy bruisability and internal hemorrhoids. Faculty teaching: initiate levothyroxine 50 mcg — mild hyperprolactinemia is likely secondary to elevated TRH from subclinical hypothyroidism; repeat pooled prolactin in 4–6 weeks after euthyroidism, not now. Simple cyst <5 cm — expectant management, repeat USG 6–8 weeks. Do not rush 13-steroid LC-MS/MS panel until thyroid and prolactin normalized. VWD type 2 constrains Meprate withdrawal bleeding and cyst rupture risk. Weight gain and LH:FSH >2 suggest PCOS phenotype exacerbated by hypothyroidism.
keyFindings: TSH 10 + normal T3/T4 + PRL 54 = secondary hyperprolactinemia until proven otherwise
Treat LT4 first → repeat pooled prolactin at 4–6 weeks; cabergoline premature
4.5 cm anechoic cyst + negative markers = benign functional cyst — repeat USG, not surgery
VWD type 2: prophylaxis for Meprate-induced HMB; counsel on cyst rupture/hemoperitoneum red flags
LH:FSH >2 + rapid weight gain → PCOS-like architecture; steroid panel deferred to phase 3
finalDiagnosis: Subclinical hypothyroidism with likely secondary hyperprolactinemia driving anovulation and irregular cycles; simple right ovarian functional cyst 4.5 cm (expectant); PCOS-phenotype hyperandrogenic/anovulatory pattern exacerbated by hypothyroidism and weight gain; VWD type 2 as primary hematologic constraint on menstrual and surgical management. Plan: LT4 50 mcg → reassess TSH and pooled PRL 4–6 weeks → repeat pelvic USG 6–8 weeks → 13-steroid panel only if cycles remain irregular after euthyroidism and normal prolactin.
learningObjectives: Link subclinical hypothyroidism to secondary hyperprolactinemia via TRH and anovulation
Sequence investigations: treat thyroid before prolactinoma workup or steroid panel
Manage simple adnexal cyst <5 cm expectantly with VWD-specific rupture counselling
Apply staged protocol avoiding diagnostic noise from confounding hyperprolactinemia
Coordinate menstrual bleeding prophylaxis in VWD type 2 when using progestins

items.title: Clinical snapshot: RTM-21 irregular cycles in subclinical hypothyroidism with VWD
items.content: 21-year-old woman: cycles irregular, delayed up to 2–3 months, withdrawal bleed to Meprate. TSH 10, normal T3/T4, no goiter. Prolactin 54. FSH 2.7, LH 5.9 (ratio >2). Weight 50→63 kg in 6 months. Right adnexal anechoic cyst 4.5 cm; AFP, CEA, CA-125, CA 19.9 negative. VWD type 2, easy bruisability, internal hemorrhoids. Resident asks what to investigate for cyst and prolactin. Faculty: start LT4 50 mcg; repeat pooled prolactin next month after euthyroidism — no point testing prolactin now; 13-steroid panel only if prolactin normalizes and cycles remain irregular.

items.title: Why subclinical hypothyroidism explains the mildly elevated prolactin
items.content: TSH 10 with normal T3/T4 is subclinical hypothyroidism. Loss of negative feedback raises TRH, which stimulates both thyrotrophs (TSH) and lactotrophs (prolactin). Prolactin 54 ng/mL is mild and fits secondary hyperprolactinemia rather than prolactinoma (which often presents >100 ng/mL with macroadenoma risk). Elevated prolactin suppresses GnRH pulsatility → anovulation → irregular cycles and functional ovarian cysts. Combined with 13 kg weight gain, this explains delayed menses and the 4.5 cm unruptured follicular cyst. Treat the thyroid first — do not start cabergoline before confirming prolactin remains elevated after euthyroidism.

items.title: How to sequence LT4, prolactin retesting, and the steroid panel
items.content: Phase 1 (now): Initiate levothyroxine 50 mcg daily. Target TSH <2.5 in reproductive-age women. Do not repeat prolactin immediately — check pooled prolactin at 4–6 weeks once euthyroid. If prolactin normalizes with TSH, secondary hyperprolactinemia is confirmed; cabergoline and pituitary MRI are unnecessary. Phase 2 (4–6 weeks): Repeat TSH, free T4, pooled prolactin; repeat pelvic USG for cyst. Phase 3 (if cycles still irregular with euthyroidism and normal prolactin): 13-steroid LC-MS/MS panel to map androgen excess pathways — differentiate PCOS, non-classic CAH, and other hyperandrogenic states. Running steroids now is confounded by hyperprolactinemia, which independently alters DHEAS and testosterone.
keyPoints: LT4 50 mcg → wait 4–6 wk for PRL.

items.title: Why the 4.5 cm anechoic cyst needs repeat ultrasound, not immediate surgery
items.content: Anechoic (simple) cyst 4.5 cm abutting the right ovary in a young woman with anovulation is overwhelmingly a benign functional/follicular cyst — persistent unruptured follicle. Negative AFP, CEA, CA-125, and CA 19.9 argue against standard epithelial ovarian malignancy patterns in this age group. Standard of care for simple cyst <5 cm: expectant management. Repeat transvaginal/transpelvic ultrasound in 6–8 weeks, ideally early in cycle after a bleed (spontaneous or Meprate-induced). No MRI, CT, or laparoscopy unless complexity develops (septations, solid components, rising CA-125, acute pain).

items.title: Why VWD type 2 changes management of Meprate and cyst complications
items.content: VWD type 2 is a qualitative von Willebrand factor defect — primary hematologic constraint. Meprate-induced withdrawal bleeds carry significant heavy menstrual bleeding risk; prophylactic tranexamic acid should be considered. Functional cysts eventually rupture or regress — in VWD type 2, rupture or hemorrhagic cyst can cause serious hemoperitoneum. Counsel red-flag acute pelvic pain → immediate evaluation. DDAVP (desmopressin): use with caution — VWD type 2B is generally contraindicated (can worsen thrombocytopenia). Confirm VWD subtype (2A, 2B, 2M, 2N) and coordinate with hematology before surgery or invasive procedures. Know severity of prior Meprate-induced bleeding.

items.title: How LH:FSH greater than 2 and weight gain suggest PCOS architecture
items.content: LH 5.9 / FSH 2.7 gives ratio >2 — classic PCOS-pattern gonadotropin discordance, often with insulin resistance. Weight gain from 50 to 63 kg in 6 months exacerbates insulin resistance, LH excess, and anovulation. Hypothyroidism independently worsens weight and cycle irregularity. After restoring euthyroidism and normalizing prolactin, if oligomenorrhea persists, evaluate for PCOS formally (Rotterdam criteria) with fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, and phase-3 steroid panel. Lifestyle intervention (diet, exercise) is critical to break the insulin-LH cycle. High-fiber diet may also help internal hemorrhoids.

items.title: Why checking prolactin now and rushing cabergoline or MRI is wrong
items.content: Resident impulse: elevated prolactin → prolactinoma workup → MRI → cabergoline. Faculty counter: with TSH 10, prolactin 54 is expected secondary elevation. Treating LT4 first tests the hypothesis cleanly. Immediate prolactin recheck adds no value on LT4 day one. Cabergoline without confirming persistent hyperprolactinemia after euthyroidism exposes the patient to unnecessary dopamine agonist side effects. MRI is reserved for prolactin >100 ng/mL, visual field defects, or persistent elevation despite normal TSH. This is sequential first-principles medicine — treat the driver, then reassess.
keyPoints: Do not chase PRL before fixing TSH.
MRI if PRL persists when euthyroid.

items.title: How levothyroxine addresses multiple complaints beyond the thyroid
items.content: LT4 50 mcg normalizes TSH, lowers TRH, and should reduce prolactin organically. Euthyroidism improves menstrual regularity, basal metabolic rate (aiding weight loss), and bowel function (may relieve constipation worsening internal hemorrhoids). No goiter on exam — subclinical hypothyroidism without structural urgency. Recheck TSH and free T4 at 4–6 weeks for dose titration. Anti-TPO antibodies optional for etiology but do not delay LT4 in symptomatic reproductive-age woman with TSH 10 and cycle disturbance.

items.title: How to implement the three-phase staged protocol
items.content: Phase 1 (current): LT4 50 mcg; VWD bleeding counselling and tranexamic acid prophylaxis for induced bleeds; lifestyle for weight; cyst rupture red flags; defer steroid panel and repeat prolactin. Phase 2 (4–6 weeks): TSH, free T4, pooled prolactin; pelvic USG for cyst resolution. Phase 3 (if indicated): 13-steroid LC-MS/MS if oligomenorrhea persists despite euthyroidism and normal prolactin — maps PCOS vs NCCAH vs other androgen excess. Hematology: confirm VWD subtype and bleeding history with Meprate.

items.title: What not to order immediately in this presentation
items.content: Avoid now: immediate repeat prolactin (wait for euthyroidism); pituitary MRI for PRL 54 with TSH 10; cabergoline; 13-steroid panel (confounded by hyperprolactinemia and hypothyroidism); laparoscopy for simple 4.5 cm cyst; extensive ovarian cancer workup beyond repeat USG with negative markers. Avoid anchoring on prolactinoma or ovarian malignancy when the unifying drivers are subclinical hypothyroidism, anovulation, and weight gain in a 21-year-old with a simple cyst.

items.question: Why should prolactin be rechecked only after 4–6 weeks of levothyroxine rather than immediately?
items.answer: Because prolactin 54 ng/mL with TSH 10 is likely secondary hyperprolactinemia from elevated TRH — not a primary lactotroph disorder. Immediate recheck on day one of LT4 cannot distinguish secondary from primary cause. After 4–6 weeks of euthyroidism, a normalized pooled prolactin confirms TRH-driven elevation; persistent elevation despite normal TSH warrants MRI and endocrine reassessment. Testing now adds cost and anxiety without actionable change.

items.question: How should the 4.5 cm anechoic adnexal cyst be investigated and monitored?
items.answer: Treat as benign simple functional cyst: expectant management with repeat transvaginal/transpelvic ultrasound in 6–8 weeks, ideally early follicular phase after a menstrual bleed. Tumor markers are already negative — no further marker panel unless imaging becomes complex. Counsel on acute pelvic pain given VWD type 2 rupture risk. Escalate to surgery only if increasing size, solid/septated features, rising CA-125, or hemodynamically significant hemorrhage — with hematology coordination for hemostasis.

items.question: Why defer the 13-steroid LC-MS/MS panel until thyroid and prolactin are normalized?
items.answer: Because hyperprolactinemia and hypothyroidism independently alter adrenal and ovarian steroidogenesis — DHEAS, testosterone, and androstenedione may be falsely skewed. The panel is meant to differentiate PCOS from non-classic CAH and other hyperandrogenic states at baseline. Running it now produces confounded results that may mislead toward NCCAH or adrenal pathology. If cycles remain irregular after euthyroidism and normal prolactin, phase-3 steroid mapping becomes diagnostically valid.

items.question: How should menstrual bleeding be managed safely in this patient with VWD type 2 who responds to Meprate?
items.answer: Confirm VWD subtype with hematology (2B affects DDAVP eligibility). Before inducing withdrawal with medroxyprogesterone, discuss heavy bleeding risk and prescribe prophylactic tranexamic acid during the bleed. Document prior Meprate-induced blood loss. Avoid unnecessary surgical intervention on the cyst. For acute pelvic pain suggestive of rupture, urgent imaging and hematology input for VWF concentrate or carefully selected hemostatic therapy — DDAVP contraindicated in type 2B. Long-term cycle regulation may prefer LT4-driven restoration of ovulation over repeated progestin withdrawal in severe bleeders.

items.stem: A 21-year-old has TSH 10 mIU/L, normal T3/T4, and prolactin 54 ng/mL with irregular cycles. What is the best first step?
options: Levothyroxine 50 mcg; repeat pooled prolactin in 4–6 weeks after euthyroidism
items.explanation: Secondary hyperprolactinemia from hypothyroidism — treat LT4 first, reassess prolactin before prolactinoma pathway.

items.stem: A 4.5 cm anechoic adnexal cyst with negative AFP, CA-125, CEA, and CA 19.9 in a 21-year-old with anovulation is best managed by:
items.explanation: Simple cyst <5 cm with negative markers — expectant management with interval USG.

items.stem: The mechanism linking TSH 10 to prolactin 54 and irregular cycles is:
options: Elevated TRH stimulating lactotrophs and suppressing GnRH pulsatility
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism → elevated TRH → mild hyperprolactinemia → anovulation.

items.stem: LH 5.9 and FSH 2.7 with 13 kg weight gain in 6 months suggests:
options: PCOS-phenotype anovulation exacerbated by weight gain and hypothyroidism
items.explanation: LH:FSH >2 with rapid weight gain — insulin-resistant PCOS architecture; hypothyroidism worsens picture.

items.stem: In VWD type 2, which concern is most important when using Meprate for withdrawal bleeding?
options: Risk of heavy menstrual bleeding; consider tranexamic acid prophylaxis
Meprate is absolutely contraindicated in all VWD
DDAVP is always mandatory before every Meprate dose regardless of subtype
items.explanation: Qualitative VWD increases HMB risk with induced bleeds; tranexamic acid and hematology coordination; DDAVP caution especially in 2B.

items.stem: When should the 13-steroid LC-MS/MS panel be ordered in this case?
options: Only if irregular cycles persist after euthyroidism and normalized prolactin
items.explanation: Phase 3 workup — after removing confounding hyperprolactinemia and hypothyroidism.

items.stem: A reasonable levothyroxine starting dose in this 21-year-old with TSH 10 is:
options: 50 mcg daily with recheck in 4–6 weeks
items.explanation: LT4 50 mcg with 4–6 week TSH titration — faculty plan for subclinical hypothyroidism with cycle disturbance.

items.stem: A patient with VWD type 2 and a functional ovarian cyst should be counselled that:
options: Cyst rupture cannot cause bleeding problems
Sudden acute pelvic pain requires urgent evaluation for hemoperitoneum
All cysts require immediate oophorectomy in VWD
items.explanation: Cyst rupture or hemorrhagic cyst can cause serious bleeding in VWD — red-flag counselling mandatory.

items.statement: Prolactin 54 ng/mL with TSH 10 always requires cabergoline before treating hypothyroidism.
items.explanation: False. Treat LT4 first — likely secondary hyperprolactinemia; reassess pooled prolactin when euthyroid.

items.statement: A simple anechoic ovarian cyst under 5 cm in a young woman typically regresses with expectant management.
items.explanation: True. Repeat USG in 6–8 weeks; negative tumor markers support benign functional cyst.

items.statement: DDAVP is universally safe in all VWD type 2 subtypes.
items.explanation: False. Type 2B — DDAVP generally contraindicated (thrombocytopenia risk); confirm subtype with hematology.

items.statement: Hyperprolactinemia can confound interpretation of adrenal and ovarian steroid panels.
items.explanation: True. Defer 13-steroid LC-MS/MS until thyroid and prolactin normalized.

items.statement: LH:FSH ratio greater than 2 supports a PCOS-like gonadotropin pattern.
items.explanation: True. LH 5.9 / FSH 2.7 in this case — with weight gain suggests PCOS phenotype.

items.statement: There is clinical value in repeating prolactin on the same day LT4 is started.
items.explanation: False. Wait 4–6 weeks on LT4 for pooled prolactin — no point testing immediately.

items.assertion: Levothyroxine should be started before pursuing prolactinoma-specific investigations in this patient.
items.reason: Mild hyperprolactinemia with TSH 10 is commonly secondary to elevated TRH from subclinical hypothyroidism and may normalize with euthyroidism.
items.explanation: Both true — treat the driver first; MRI/cabergoline only if prolactin persists when euthyroid.

items.assertion: Induced withdrawal bleeding with Meprate requires special precaution in this patient.
items.reason: Von Willebrand disease type 2 impairs hemostasis and increases the risk of heavy menstrual bleeding.
items.explanation: Both true — tranexamic acid prophylaxis and hematology coordination; document prior bleed severity.
